blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلتِّينِ وَٱلزَّيْتُونِ (١)
By the fig and the olive, (1)
وَطُورِ سِينِينَ (٢)
by Mount Sinai, (2)
وَهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ ٱلْأَمِينِ (٣)
and by this secure town: (3)
لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِىٓ أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ (٤)
We certainly created man in the best of forms; (4)
ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَـٰهُ أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ (٥)
then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, (5)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ (٦)
except those who have faith and do righteous deeds. There will be an everlasting reward for them. (6)
فَمَا يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِٱلدِّينِ (٧)
So what makes you deny the Retribution? (7)
أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَحْكَمِ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨)
Is not Allah the fairest of all judges? (8)

Content and Virtue of Surah At-Tin

This Surah essentially revolves around the axis of the beautiful creation of man, and the stages of his perfection and decline. This subject begins with meaningful oaths at the start of the Surah, and after enumerating the factors for man's victory and salvation, it ultimately concludes by emphasizing the issue of the Resurrection and the absolute sovereignty of Allah.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Whoever recites this Surah, Allah will grant him two qualities as long as he is in this world: well-being (afiyah) and certainty (yaqin). And when he passes away, Allah will grant him a reward equal to the reward of one day of fasting for every person who has recited this Surah." [1] This Surah was revealed in Mecca, and the verse "And [by] this secure city" (وَ هذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ), which refers to the city of Mecca using a near demonstrative pronoun, is evidence of this.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 510.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

By the fig and the olive (1) And [by] Mount Sinai (2) And [by] this secure city (3) We have certainly created man in the best of statures (4) Then We return him to the lowest of the low (5) Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds, for they will have a reward uninterrupted (6) So what yet causes you to deny the Recompense? (7) Is not Allah the most just of judges? (8)

Commentary

We Created Man in the Best Form

At the beginning of this Surah, we encounter four meaningful oaths that serve as an introduction to a highly significant concept.

It says: "By the fig and the olive" (وَ التِّينِ وَ الزَّيْتُونِ).

"And [by] Mount Sinai" (وَ طُورِ سِينِينَ) [1]

[1]: Some have considered "Sinin" to be the plural of "Sinah" meaning "tree," and given that "Tur" means "mountain," the concept becomes a mountain full of trees. Others have said "Sinin" is the name of the land where that mountain is located. Some have also said "Sinin" means blessed and beautiful, and it is a word in the language of the people of Abyssinia (Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 30, p. 173).

"And [by] this secure city" (وَ هذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ).

"Tin" literally means "fig," and "Zaytun" is the well-known olive from which a useful oily substance is extracted.

Regarding whether the intent is an oath by these two famous fruits or something else, commentators have various views.

While some consider it a reference to the two famous fruits themselves, which possess extraordinary nutritional and medicinal properties, others believe it refers to the two mountains upon which the cities of Damascus and Jerusalem are situated. This is because these two locations are the lands where many great Prophets of Allah arose, and these two oaths would thus harmonize with the third and fourth oaths which mention sacred lands.

Some have also said that naming these two mountains "Tin" and "Zaytun" is because fig trees grow on one and olive trees on the other.

Others have considered "Tin" to be a reference to the era of Adam, as the clothing Adam and Eve wore in Paradise was from fig leaves, and "Zaytun" to be a reference to the era of Noah, because in the final stages of the Great Flood, he released a dove to search for dry land appearing from under the water; it returned with an olive branch, and Noah understood that the flood had ended and land had emerged (hence the olive branch is a symbol of peace and security).

Some also consider "Tin" to be a reference to the Mosque of Noah built on Mount Judi, and "Zaytun" to be a reference to Jerusalem.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse at first glance is indeed those two famous fruits, but considering the subsequent oaths, a reference to two mountains or two sacred and respected centers is more appropriate.

Thus, in a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that Allah chose four cities from among all cities and said regarding them:

وَ التِّينِ وَ الزَّيْتُونِ وَ طُورِ سِينِينَ وَ هذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ

"Tin is Medina, Zaytun is Jerusalem, Tur Sinin is Kufa, and 'this secure city' is Mecca." [1] The term "Tur Sinin" apparently refers to "Tur Sina" (Mount Sinai), which commentators have taken as a reference to the famous Mount Tur in the Sinai Peninsula, where productive olive trees exist.

"Sina" is understood to mean a blessed, tree-filled, or beautiful mountain, and it is the same mountain where Moses would go for intimate prayer (munajat).

Some have also considered it a mountain near Kufa and the land of Najaf.

Some have explicitly stated that "Sinin" and "Sina" are one and the same, meaning blessed.

As for "And [by] this secure city" (وَ هذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ), it certainly refers to the land of Mecca—a land that even in the Age of Ignorance was considered a secure zone and the Sanctuary (Haram) of Allah, where no one had the right to transgress against another. Even criminals and murderers were safe when they reached that land.

This land holds extraordinary importance in Islam; its animals, trees, and birds—let alone its human beings—must enjoy a specific security there. [1] It is also noteworthy that the word "Tin" is mentioned only here in the Holy Quran, while the word "Zaytun" appears explicitly six times in the Holy Quran and once by implication, where He says: "And a tree that grows from Mount Sinai which produces oil and a relish for those who eat" (Al-Mu'minun: 20).

If we take these two oaths (the fig and the olive) in their primary sense—meaning the well-known fig and olive—it is still a very meaningful oath because: "The fig" possesses great nutritional value and is a nourishing and strengthening morsel for every age, free from skin, pits, or waste.

Nutritionists say: Figs can be used as a natural sugar for children. Athletes and those suffering from weakness or old age can use figs for their nutrition.

It is said that Plato loved figs so much that some called it "the friend of philosophers," and Socrates considered the fig an absorber of beneficial substances and an expeller of harmful ones.

Galen devised a special diet of figs for athletes; the athletes of ancient Rome and Greece were also given figs.

Nutritionists say figs are rich in various vitamins and sugar, and can be used as a medicine for many diseases. Especially when figs and honey are mixed in equal parts, it is very beneficial for stomach ulcers. Eating dried figs strengthens thought. In short, due to the presence of mineral elements in figs that cause balance in the body's forces and blood, figs have been introduced as a food for every age and condition.

In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated: "The fig removes bad breath, strengthens the mouth and bones, makes hair grow, eliminates pain, and with it, there is no need for medicine." He also said: "The fig is the thing most similar to the plants of Paradise." [1] [2] As for the "olive," nutritionists and great scientists who have spent many years of their lives studying the various properties of fruits place extraordinary importance on the olive and its oil. They believe that those who wish to remain healthy should use this elixir of life.

Olive oil is a close friend of the human liver, and it is very effective in relieving kidney ailments, gallstones, renal and hepatic colic, and constipation.

For this reason, the olive tree is mentioned as a "blessed tree" in the Holy Quran.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 27, p. 143

Olive oil is also rich in various vitamins and contains phosphorus, sulfur, calcium, iron, potassium, and manganese.

Ointments prepared from olive oil and garlic have been found beneficial for various rheumatic pains. Gallstones are eliminated through the consumption of olive oil. [1] In a report from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "A house is never poor where they use vinegar and olive oil as a relish; this was the food of the Prophets." [2] And in a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated: "Olive oil is a good food: it sweetens the breath, removes phlegm, clears the complexion, strengthens the nerves, eliminates illness, pain, and weakness, and extinguishes the fire of anger." [3] We conclude this discussion with a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who said: "Eat olive oil and anoint yourselves with it, for it is from a blessed tree." [1]

After mentioning these four content-rich oaths, the response to the oaths is given as follows: "We have certainly created man in the best of statures" (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ). "Taqwim" means to bring something into a suitable form, a balanced system, and a worthy quality. The breadth of its meaning indicates that Allah created man balanced and worthy in every respect—physically, spiritually, and intellectually. He placed every kind of potential within him and prepared him to traverse a very great ascending arc. Although man is a "small body," He has placed the "great world" (macrocosm) within him and granted him so many merits that he became worthy of the robe of: "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam" (Surah Al-Isra, Verse 70). This is the same human being about whom He says after completing his creation: "So blessed is Allah, the best of creators!"

Yet this same human being, with all these advantages, if he deviates from the path of truth, falls so far that he is dragged to the "lowest of the low." Thus, in the next verse, He says: "Then We return him to the lowest of the low" (ثُمَّ رَدَدْناهُ أَسْفَلَ سافِلِينَ).

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 606, Hadith 4. It is true that at that time "Kufa" was not a large city, but given the passage of the Euphrates River through that land, there were certainly many settlements there. "It is also gathered from histories that a city existed there before Islam as well" (Dairat al-Ma'arif Musahib, Vol. 2, entry: Kufa).

[1]: The word "Amin" here may be fa'il in the sense of fa'il (active), meaning "possessor of security," or fa'il in the sense of maf'ul (passive), meaning a land in which people are secure.

[1]: Kafi, Vol. 6, p. 358. The late Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 66, p. 184, has brought several narrations regarding the properties of figs. [2]: The First University and the Last Prophet, Vol. 9, p. 90 onwards.

[1]: The First University and the Last Prophet, Vol. 9, p. 130 onwards. [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 66, p. 180, Hadith 6. [3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 66, p. 183, Hadith 22.

[1]: Ibid, p. 182, Hadith 16.

It is said that there are always very deep valleys alongside high mountains. Corresponding to that ascending arc of human perfection, a terrifying descending arc is seen. Why should it not be so? For he is a being full of abundant talents; if he uses them in the way of righteousness, he stands upon the highest peak of glory, but if he employs all this intelligence and talent in the way of corruption, he creates the greatest mischief, and it is natural that he be dragged to the "lowest of the low."

But in the next verse, He adds: "Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds, for they will have a reward uninterrupted" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ). "Mamnun" is from the root mann, which here means interruption or deficiency. Therefore, "ghayru mamnun" refers to a reward that is permanent and free from any deficiency. Some have said it means being free from any "favor" (obligation) being held over them, but the first meaning seems more appropriate.

Some have interpreted the phrase "Then We return him to the lowest of the low" as the weakness, frailty, and extreme loss of intelligence during old age. However, in that case, it would not be consistent with the exception in the following verse. Therefore, considering the totality of the preceding and succeeding verses, the first interpretation seems correct.

In the next verse, addressing this ungrateful human who is indifferent to the proofs and signs of the Resurrection, He says: "So what yet causes you to deny the Recompense?" (فَما يُكَذِّبُكَ بعدُ بِالدِّينِ). The structure of your own being on one hand, and the structure of this vast universe on the other, show that the few days of worldly life cannot be the ultimate goal of your creation and this great world.

These are all a prelude to a wider and more perfect world. As the Quran expresses, the "first creation" itself informs of the "latter creation"; why does man not take heed? "And you have already known the first creation, so why do you not take heed?" (Al-Waqi'ah: 62). [1] The world of plants constantly and every year visualizes the scene of death and renewed life before man's eyes, and the successive creations during the embryonic stages are each considered a resurrection and a renewed life. Despite all this, how can this human deny the Day of Recompense?

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the addressee in this verse is the human species. The possibility that "the addressee is the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the meaning is: who or what can deny you despite the proofs of the Resurrection?" seems unlikely.

It also became clear that "Din" here does not mean religion or law, but rather the Recompense and the Day of Judgment. The next verse also testifies to this meaning.

As He says: "Is not Allah the most just of judges?" (أَ لَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِأَحْكَمِ الْحاكِمِينَ). And if we take "Din" to mean the entire Sharia and religion, the meaning of this verse would be: "Are not Allah's laws and commands the most wise and firm of all?" Or that the creation of the Lord regarding man is in every respect mixed with wisdom, knowledge, and planning.

However, as we said, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

[1]: In Vol. 23, p. 239 onwards, seven proofs for the issue of "Resurrection" are explained using the verses of Surah Al-Waqi'ah.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that when the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited Surah At-Tin and reached the verse "Is not Allah the most just of judges?", he would say:

"Yes, and I am among the witnesses to that." (بلى و انا على ذلك من الشاهدين) [1]

O Allah! We too bear witness that You are the most just of judges. O Lord! You have placed our creation in the best of forms; grant us the success so that our actions and ethics may also be in the best of forms. O God! Traversing the path of faith and righteous deeds is not possible except by Your grace; include us in Your favors on this path.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah At-Tin.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 512. The same theme appears in the commentaries of Ruh al-Bayan, Qurtubi, and Fi Zilal under the verse in question.
